diagrams.description: This timeline traces the history of Cognitive Psychology. Early precursors include Donders’ 1868 mental chronometry and Ebbinghaus’ 1885 studies of memory. Bartlett’s 1932 work on reconstructive memory challenged behaviorist views. The cognitive revolution gained momentum with the 1948 Hixon Symposium and Miller’s 1956 ‘magical number 7’. Chomsky’s 1959 review of Skinner’s verbal behavior marked a critical break. Key foundational models emerged: Broadbent’s 1958 filter model, Neisser’s 1967 textbook defining the field, and Atkinson & Shiffrin’s 1968 multi‑store model. The 1970s saw Baddeley & Hitch’s working memory model (1974) and Kahneman & Tversky’s heuristics (1974). The 1980s brought connectionism (Rumelhart & McClelland, 1986), while the 1990s saw cognitive neuroscience flourish. Since 2000, embodied cognition and big data approaches have expanded the field.

timeline title History of Cognitive Psychology section Precursors 1868 : Donders
Mental chronometry (reaction time) 1885 : Ebbinghaus
Experimental study of memory 1932 : Bartlett
Reconstructive memory section Cognitive Revolution 1948 : Hixon Symposium
Cerebral mechanisms 1956 : George Miller
"Magical number 7" 1958 : Broadbent
Filter model of attention 1959 : Noam Chomsky
Review of Skinner's verbal behavior section Foundational Models 1967 : Ulric Neisser
Cognitive Psychology (textbook) 1968 : Atkinson & Shiffrin
Multi-store model of memory 1974 : Baddeley & Hitch
Working memory model 1974 : Kahneman & Tversky
Heuristics and biases section Expansion 1986 : Rumelhart & McClelland
Connectionism (PDP) 1990s : Cognitive neuroscience

mirror neurons 2010s : Big data & computational
